metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an in-repo artifact option for the RED-evidence capture in AC3 (e.g. a committed file `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/orchestrate/proving_step_evidence.txt` containing the leaf id, injected defect, and captured failing output), so `git log` / CI can verify the evidence exists without accessing an external ticketing system.
findings.finding: AC3's durable evidence — a 'rebar comment on this ticket' — is an external-to-repo artifact with no in-repo proving command, so no CI step or `make test` invocation can verify this AC was completed; the plan acknowledges `make test` is 'not sufficient' but proposes no alternative automated or repo-resident check.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC3 to require the RED-run output to be committed to the repo (e.g. as `tests/.../proving_step_evidence.txt`) AND optionally also posted as a rebar comment; add a CI lint step or a `test -f` guard in the Makefile that fails if the file is absent, making the evidence verifiable in-session.

findings.finding: AC3 (PROVING STEP) requires the RED mutation to be 'demonstrated, not assumed', but specifies no concrete mechanism to capture or evidence the RED state — an executor who reverts the mutation and shows only a green `make test` has satisfied the letter but not the spirit of the criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Evidence of the RED run must appear in the PR description or a linked CI artifact (e.g., paste of `pytest -x tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/orchestrate/test_leaves_registry_coverage.py 2>&1 | tail -20` output showing at least one FAILED line for the defect-injected leaf, run BEFORE the revert commit).' This pins the proving command and the artifact.

findings.finding: AC #3's PROVING STEP requires the RED mutation to be 'demonstrated, not assumed,' but specifies no protocol or mechanism for capturing/evidencing the RED state — an executor who reverts the defect and re-runs `make test` green has no way to prove the RED phase was actually reached.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a minimum behavioral assertion standard for 'thin' matrix entries covering already-tested leaves (e.g., must call the leaf function and assert at least one observable return value or mock call, not merely reference an external test by comment).
findings.finding: The plan permits 'thin assertions' for already-covered leaves (entries that 'reference the same observable effect' or fold in existing tests), but does not define a minimum behavioral assertion standard for such entries. This escape hatch allows a developer to satisfy the completeness check with a stub or comment pointer that passes without actually asserting any observable effect in the matrix, undermining the plan's stated goal of 'one complete, enumerated behavioral matrix over _LEAVES.'
findings.location: What section: 'Where a leaf is already behaviorally covered, the matrix entry may be a thin assertion that references the same observable effect (or the existing test is folded into the matrix)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 or the What section: 'For leaves already covered elsewhere, the matrix entry MUST still invoke the leaf with a mock client and assert at least its success-path ApplyResult; a pointer-only entry (comment, skip, or bare pass) does not satisfy the matrix requirement.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the minimum bar for a 'thin' matrix entry: e.g. 'must assert at least the returned `ApplyResult.status` and that the expected mock method was called once, even when a deeper test exists elsewhere.'
findings.finding: The plan does not define what 'thin assertion' means for already-covered leaves, leaving the minimum acceptable matrix entry for those leaves undefined; an agent cannot know whether a single `assert result is not None` satisfies 'thin' or whether it requires matching the same effect dimensions as fully-covered leaves.
findings.location: What section — 'the matrix entry may be a thin assertion that references the same observable effect'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'What' section: 'A thin entry must, at minimum, assert the expected `ApplyResult` status and verify the primary mock call was invoked; it may omit error-path and follow-on assertions when those are fully covered by the referenced existing test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description of the proving step to the What or Scope section so it appears as explicit required work (not only as an acceptance criterion), including who performs it, when (e.g., before PR is opened), and what artifact is produced.
findings.finding: AC3 (the PROVING STEP — inject a defect, run the test, capture the RED output, record it as a rebar comment, then revert) has no corresponding described work in the What or Scope sections. It is an acceptance criterion with no plan-body description of how/when it will be performed, making it an orphan criterion.
findings.location: AC3 vs. What and Scope sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or What: 'Before opening the PR, the author must: (1) temporarily inject one of the listed defects into a representative leaf\'s production body, (2) run that leaf\'s matrix test and capture the failing output, (3) post the leaf id, defect injected, and failure output as a rebar comment on this ticket, and (4) revert the production change. This step is a prerequisite for AC3 sign-off.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile What and AC2: either (a) amend AC2 to allow reference/fold entries for error paths when an existing behavioral test fully covers that path (and define 'fully covers'), or (b) amend What to require error-path entries to be direct assertions in the matrix regardless of existing coverage.
findings.finding: The What section permits 'thin assertion that references the same observable effect' for leaves already covered elsewhere, but AC2 requires 'EVERY leaf that has an error/exception path asserts that path's observable behavior' with no exemption for already-covered leaves. An implementer following What may produce a reference-only entry for an already-covered leaf's error path, while an implementer following AC2 would require a direct assertion — these two sections send the implementer in opposite directions.
findings.location: What section ('the matrix entry may be a thin assertion that references the same observable effect') vs. AC2 ('EVERY leaf that has an error/exception path ... asserts that path's observable behavior')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clarifying sentence to AC2: 'For leaves whose error path is already fully asserted in an existing behavioral test that is explicitly folded into (or cross-referenced from) the matrix entry, the cross-reference satisfies this criterion; a thin reference without a foldable existing assertion does not.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'thin entry' ambiguity: define the minimum behavioral bar a matrix entry must meet for a leaf that is already covered in a separate test module — e.g., 'a thin entry must contain at least one client-mock call assertion and one ApplyResult field assertion, OR explicitly cite the covering test file and the specific assertion(s) it contains' — so AC1 (completeness) and AC2 (behavioral assertion) are jointly satisfiable without redundancy.
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously allows 'thin assertions' for already-covered leaves (referencing the same observable effect) and requires every matrix entry to 'assert its observable effect via a mock Jira client.' These two clauses are unresolved when applied to the 7 inbound leaves in test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py and the outbound leaves in test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py: an executor can satisfy the completeness AC with a stub-pointer entry that contains no client-mock assertion, violating AC2 — or write fully redundant behavioral tests duplicating existing coverage. Without a concrete definition of what a valid 'thin entry' looks like (minimum assertion level), the plan leaves the resolution to each leaf to the executor's discretion, creating an internal contradiction that can produce a matrix that trivially passes completeness while being behaviorally hollow for pre-covered leaves.
findings.location: What section ('the matrix entry may be a thin assertion that references the same observable effect') and AC2 ('Each leaf's entry asserts its observable effect via a mock Jira client')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clarifying sentence to the What section: 'A thin matrix entry for an already-covered leaf must include at minimum (a) a reference to the canonical covering test and its assertion, and (b) one smoke-level call assertion (e.g., `assert result.direction == expected_direction`) directly in the matrix — ensuring the matrix row is not a no-op registry pointer while avoiding full duplication of the covering test.'

findings.finding: The What section permits 'thin assertions' for already-behaviorally-covered leaves, but AC #2 mandates that EACH entry assert all four observable effects (exact client/store call + key args, ApplyResult, follow-ons, failure behavior). An implementer may legitimately read 'thin assertion' as license to omit failure-behavior or follow-on assertions for leaves already covered elsewhere, directly contradicting AC #2's completeness requirement.
findings.location: What section ('the matrix entry may be a thin assertion') vs AC #2 ('Each leaf's entry asserts its observable effect… follow-ons, and failure behavior')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the matrix entry may be a thin assertion that references the same observable effect' with 'the matrix entry must cover all four observable dimensions (call, ApplyResult, follow-ons, failure behavior), but may delegate to or reuse an existing test fixture rather than duplicating setup code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `test_apply_outbound_*` wildcard citation with an explicit enumeration of the test files that already provide behavioral coverage per leaf (e.g., `test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py` → outbound_create, `test_outbound_update_combined.py` → outbound_update, etc.) so the 'thin entry allowed' exclusion is grounded in verifiable, per-leaf evidence.
findings.finding: The plan's scope-exclusion assertion 'several leaves already have behavioral tests elsewhere (e.g. `test_apply_outbound_*`)' implies a `test_apply_outbound_<leaf>.py` file-naming pattern covering multiple outbound leaves, but a repo-wide search finds exactly ONE file matching that pattern: `test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py`. The other behavioral tests for outbound leaves (e.g., `test_outbound_update_combined.py`, `test_outbound_create_binding.py`, `test_update_one_field_filter.py`) use entirely different naming conventions. An executor using the cited pattern to identify 'already covered' leaves would misidentify coverage scope.
findings.location: What section — 'Do not blindly duplicate existing coverage: several leaves already have behavioral tests elsewhere (e.g. `test_apply_outbound_*`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, replace 'e.g. `test_apply_outbound_*`' with an explicit table: leaf id → existing behavioral test file → what it covers. This makes the scope exclusion verifiable and prevents both over- and under-testing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the claim that `test_apply_outbound_*` already provides behavioral coverage for outbound leaves: only `test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py` exists; enumerate which outbound leaves (_apply_outbound_delete, _apply_outbound_probe, _apply_outbound_conflict) have NO existing behavioral test and must receive full matrix entries.
findings.finding: The What section uses the plural `test_apply_outbound_*` to characterize existing behavioral coverage for outbound leaves, implying multiple such files exist. In reality only one file covers an outbound leaf behaviorally: `test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py` (covering the rollback path of `_apply_outbound_create`). The leaves `_apply_outbound_delete`, `_apply_outbound_probe`, and `_apply_outbound_conflict` have no dedicated behavioral test files; `_apply_outbound_update` has partial coverage in `test_outbound_update_combined.py`. An executor relying on the plural framing may assume outbound leaves are already 'behaviorally covered' and produce only thin/tautological matrix entries for them, leaving real behavioral gaps.
findings.location: What section — 'several leaves already have behavioral tests elsewhere (e.g. `test_apply_outbound_*`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'e.g. `test_apply_outbound_*`' with a precise enumeration: 'e.g. `test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py` (covers _apply_outbound_create rollback) and `test_outbound_update_combined.py` (covers _apply_outbound_update success path). Leaves _apply_outbound_delete, _apply_outbound_probe, and _apply_outbound_conflict have no existing behavioral tests and require full matrix entries.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default file-layout decision: either extend `test_leaves_registry_coverage.py` in-place OR create a named new module (e.g. `test_leaves_behavioral_matrix.py`), removing the open 'and/or' choice.
findings.finding: The file-structure decision for the behavioral matrix ('`tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/orchestrate/test_leaves_registry_coverage.py` (and/or a new per-leaf matrix module)') is left open to the executor, which means an agent must make an architectural choice the plan defers rather than decides.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'and/or a new per-leaf matrix module' with a stated default, e.g. 'extend the existing file unless it exceeds 400 lines, in which case split into `test_leaves_behavioral_matrix.py`.'

findings.finding: AC2 requires each leaf's entry to assert 'failure behavior' but does not quantify the minimum — no stated requirement for which error paths must be covered or how many, leaving this criterion evaluable only subjectively.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC2 to read: '…and failure behavior (at minimum: the mock raises the primary exception the leaf is expected to handle or propagate, and the test asserts the resulting `ApplyResult.status` or that the exception is re-raised with the correct type).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
